coredata.dc:title: High enrichment and sensitive measurement of oxytetracycline in tea drinks by thermosensitive magnetic molecular imprinting based magnetic solid phase extraction coupled with boron doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhi
Li, Shuangying
Jiang, Liushan
Niu, Jingwen
Zhang, Yue
Nie, Linchun
Zhou, Qingxiang
Chen, Chunmao
coredata.dc:description: As a typical kind of new pollutants, there are still some challenges in the rapid detection of antibiotics. In this work, a sensitive fluorescent probe based on boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) in combination with thermo-responsive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) was constructed for the detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) in tea drinks. T-MMIPs were designed, fabricated and employed to enrich OTC at trace level from tea drinks, and B-CDs were utilized as the fluorescent probe to detect the concentration of OTC. The proposed method exhibited good linear relationship with OTC concentration from 0.2 to 60 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.1 μg L−1. The established method has been successfully validated with tea beverages. Present work was the first attempt application of T-MMIPs in combination with CDs in detection of OTC, and demonstrated that the proposed method endowed the detection of OTC with high selectivity, sensitivity, reliability and wide application prospect, meanwhile offered a new strategy for the method establishment of rapid and sensitive detection of trace antibiotics in food and other matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thermosensitive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers
Boron doped carbon dots
Oxytetracycline
Fluorescence spectrometry
Magnetic solid phase extraction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence quenching mechanism of boron doped carbon dots
Optimization of important parameters for enrichment
Optimization of kind of eluent, eluent volume and elution time
Optimization of adsorption time and amount of T-MMIPs
Optimization of adsorption temperature and elution temperature
Sensitivity and selectivity of the established method
Reproducibility and stability of the established method

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM of B-CDs; (B) TEM of Fe3O4; (C) TEM of T-MMIPs; (D) Fourier infrared spectra of Fe3O4 and T-MMIPs; (E) Fourier infrared spectrum of B-CDs; (F) XRD patterns of Fe3O4 and T-MMIPs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs with different excitation wavelengths. (B) Effect of the pH on the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs. (C) Effect of incubation time on fluorescence intensity of B-CDs. (D) Fluorescence stability of B-CDs. (E) UV–visible absorption spectra of B-CDs and OTC. (F) Absorption spectrum of OTC, excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs. (G) Fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs, and B-CDs + OTC.

Optimization of MSPE parameters. (A) Eluent selection, (B) Optimization of eluent volume, (C) Optimization of elution time, (D) Effect of adsorption time, (E) Optimization of adsorbent dosage, (F) Effect of pH, (G) Effect of adsorption temperature, (H) Effect of elution temperature, (I) Effect of sample volume, (J) Optimization of salting-out effect.
Effect of fluorescence detection conditions. (A) Optimization of B-CDs concentration, (B) Optimization of pH.

(A) Fluorescence response of OTC at different concentrations to B-CDs, (B) Linear range of the proposed method, (C) The adsorption performance and selectivity of T-MMIPs for six antibiotics, (D) The adsorption performance and selectivity of T-MNIPs for six antibiotics.
Analytical results of three actual tea samples with established method.

head.ce:title: High enrichment and sensitive measurement of oxytetracycline in tea drinks by thermosensitive magnetic molecular imprinting based magnetic solid phase extraction coupled with boron doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical Engineering and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Chemical Engineering and Environment, China University of Petroleum-Beijing, Beijing 102249, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a typical kind of new pollutants, there are still some challenges in the rapid detection of antibiotics. In this work, a sensitive fluorescent probe based on boron-doped carbon dots (B-CDs) in combination with thermo-responsive magnetic molecularly imprinted polymers (T-MMIPs) was constructed for the detection of oxytetracycline (OTC) in tea drinks. T-MMIPs were designed, fabricated and employed to enrich OTC at trace level from tea drinks, and B-CDs were utilized as the fluorescent probe to detect the concentration of OTC. The proposed method exhibited good linear relationship with OTC concentration from 0.2 to 60 μg L−1 and the limit of detection was 0.1 μg L−1. The established method has been successfully validated with tea beverages. Present work was the first attempt application of T-MMIPs in combination with CDs in detection of OTC, and demonstrated that the proposed method endowed the detection of OTC with high selectivity, sensitivity, reliability and wide application prospect, meanwhile offered a new strategy for the method establishment of rapid and sensitive detection of trace antibiotics in food and other matrices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have achieved great consumption in the past due to their good properties (Leal, Santos, & Esteves, 2019), however the abuse of antibiotics can enhance bacterial resistance, thereby reducing human immune function. In addition, antibiotics may also cause allergic reactions in the body, posing a serious threat to human health (Dai et al., 2019; Ouyang, Yang, Xu, Wang, & Xie, 2021; Sun, Zhang, & Aboul-Enein, 2004; Zhang et al., 2023), which is because antibiotics are structurally stable, and may be accumulated in meat, eggs, milk and other foods, and further endanger human health through the food chain and ecological cycle. In addition, the intake of antibiotics at lower doses will have an impact on human health. Oxytetracycline (OTC) is one of the typical antibiotics, and a broad-spectrum tetracycline antibiotic with advantages such as low cost, strong antibacterial activity, and convenient application. However, the excessive use leads to discharge of OTC into environment. The WHO stipulated that the acceptable uptake of oxytetracycline (OTC) was 0.15 μg g−1 per day, and the maximum residue limit in food was 0.1 μg g−1 (Ling et al., 2023). Thus, in order to improve food safety and protect human health from antibiotics, it is of great significance to establish a sensitive and selective probe for the detection of OTC.

At present, the commonly used detection technologies for OTC include liquid chromatography (Castillo-García, Aguilar-Caballos, & Gómez-Hens, 2015), ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Castellari, Gratacós-Cubarsí, & García-Regueiro, 2009), Raman spectroscopy (Lee et al., 2020), spectrophotometry (Kowalski, Pomorska, & Sławik, 2006), electrochemical analysis (Ouyang et al., 2017), etc. nevertheless, some of them have defects such as high cost, tedious operations, long analytical time, and inability to detect on-site, which limit their practical application (Yang et al., 2021). In contrast, fluorescence spectroscopy (FL) not only earns merits of fast reaction time, inexpensiveness, and easy to operate, but also has satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity, making it a feasible quantitative analysis method for OTC. Especially, the emerging of carbon dots (CDs), an emerging twinkling star in analytical field, starts a new era of fluorescence analysis. CDs are a new carbonaceous nanomaterial and achieve much more attentions from researchers since it was introduced, and have been often explored as fluorescent probes for many pollutants due to their extensive precursors, low cost, ease of preparation, low toxicity, good water solubility, photostability as well as biocompatibility (Li et al., 2022a; Li et al., 2022b; Li et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023; Li et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023). CDs possess great capacity as highly selective monitoring tool for the quantitative determination of antibiotics.

Generally, the residual amount of OTC in food samples such as tea, meat, eggs and so on is very low, and the matrices are relatively complex, which may cause trouble for the accurate detection of OTC. Therefore, it is necessary to select appropriate pre-treatment methods for enrichment, separation, and purification of the samples before fluorescence detection. The common sample pre-treatment methods currently involve conventional liquid-liquid extraction, solid phase extraction (SPE), solid phase microextraction, and magnetic solid phase extraction (MSPE) (Chen et al., 2022; Tong et al., 2023), etc. MSPE technology is a pre-treatment technology derived from SPE technology (Li et al., 2020), and its adsorption mechanism is as the same as SPE. The difference is that the adsorbent of MSPE is magnetic adsorbents and which can be easily separated by applying a magnet. However, Fe3O4, commonly used as the magnetic cores, has fewer surface functional groups, and its direct use as the adsorbents may lead to problems including easy agglomeration and lower adsorption capacity for the target analytes. Therefore, modification to improve the adsorption properties of the magnetic materials has become a valuable direction in separation field. Many new functionalized-materials including temperature-responsive materials, polymers, carbonaceous materials and metal organic frameworks have been successfully decorated onto Fe3O4. Molecular imprinting technology is also an important common modification method for Fe3O4, which not only retains the magnetic characteristics of the composite but also introduces advantages of molecular imprinting (Ma et al., 2023; Xia, Wang, Wu, & Wang, 2023). In order to further improve extraction efficiency, temperature sensitive materials were innovatively introduced into magnetic molecular imprinting to synthesize thermosensitive magnetic molecular imprinting (T-MMIPs). The water solubility of modification material of composite material decreases as the temperature increases. When the temperature reaches a certain temperature, it becomes turbid by coiling to globules, and when the temperature falls below this temperature, it turns into clear solution again which can be used for elution. This phase change temperature is known as the low critical solubility temperature (LCST) (Guo et al., 2022; Xiong et al., 2022). By changing the extraction temperature and controlling the volume of the imprinting chamber inside T-MMIPs, the extraction efficiency of the target substance can be further improved. Therefore, the combination of T-MMIPs and CDs is a creative attempt for detecting OTC in food, which may significantly increase clean-up performance and improve the sensitivity and selectivity of the proposed method.

In this work, the aim was to establish a new method for OTC detection in tea drinks basing on the T-MMIPs in combination with B-CDs. Boron doped carbon dots (B-CDs) prepared by hydrothermal method were used as specific fluorescent probe for detection of OTC. T-MMIPs were synthesized with simple methods and utilized as adsorbents in the MSPE process to enrich OTC in tea samples. The key factors for enrichment and detection were explored in detail, and the sensitivity and selectivity of the established method were evaluated. In addition, the established method was also used to detect antibiotics in tea drinks.

Oxytetracycline, ciprofloxacin, chlortetracycline, lincomycin, tetracycline, enrofloxacin, n-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), tetraethyl silicate (TEOS), ferric chloride, sodium dodecylbenzene sulfonate, sodium acetate, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate, ammonia, 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN), nicotinic acid and sodium hydroxide were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The other reagents and instruments used for measurement were provided in the supporting information.

B-CDs were fabricated with a hydrothermal method with 0.2 g nicotinic acid and 0.1 g boric acid, as the precursors, and their aqueous solution (15 mL) were put into the PTFE autoclave, which was placed in a drying oven with temperature controlled at 180 °C for 8 h. After that, the obtained products were centrifuged (6000 rpm) and filtered with 0.22 μm filter membrane. The supernatant was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (molecular cutoff, 1000 Da) for one day. At last, the final solution was dried for 24 h to gain B-CDs powders.

Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@SiO2 were fabricated according to the method described in our previous report (Li, Zhou, et al., 2023). As Fe3O4@SiO2 was concerned, only the initial amount was changed to 1 g, and the rest was as the same in the report.

T-MMIPs was synthesized as following. Oxytetracycline (0.1 g) and NIPAM (0.5 g) were completely mixed in acetonitrile (100 mL). The solution was kept for 24 h at ambient temperature for prepolymerization, and then Fe3O4@SiO2 (1 g) was added. Further ethylene dimethacrylate (2 g) and AIBN (0.05 g) were fortified in the aforementioned solution. The solution was kept with stirring at 60 °C for half one day. When the solution was cooled to ambient temperature, the final products were separated and cleaned using methanol until OTC was not checked in the washing solution by UV–Vis method. The achieved nanomaterials were dried in vacuum at 40 °C for one day. Meanwhile, T-MNIPs were also prepared with the same method except no oxytetracycline addition during the pre-polymerization stage of T-MMIPs, and the other processes were consistent to synthesize T-MMIPs. TMNIPs were employed as a referee for evaluating the adsorption performance of subsequent T-MMIPs.

For validating the feasibility of the constructed method, iced black tea, jasmine green tea and green tea were purchased from local supermarket. These tea drinks were filtered through 0.22 μm membrane and kept at 4 °C for further use.

T-MMIPs materials (80 mg) were dispersed in the sample (50 mL) containing OTC at 20 μg L−1 and then shaken at 32.5 °C for 1 h (200 rpm). Further the magnetic TMMIPs were taken out by applying a magnet. After that, the adsorbed OTC was eluted from T-MMIPs with 10 mL methanol (three times, four minutes each) at 22.5 °C. The eluent was dried with N2 stream and the residual was dissolved with methanol (50 μL), buffer (940 μL, pH 4) and CDs solution (10 μL, 1.4 mg mL−1). After mixing for 4 min, the fluorescence intensity of the final solution was recorded keeping excitation and emission wavelengths at 320 nm and 378 nm, respectively.

Fig. 1A demonstrates that B-CDs possess spheroid structure with uniform size distribution, with a mean grain size of 7.51 nm. Fig. 1B shows that Fe3O4 has a spheroid structure with a mean grain size of 312 nm. Fig. 1C clearly exhibits the presence of a transparent coating on the surface of T-MMIPs, with a mean grain size of 499 nm, that is significantly greater than that of Fe3O4, which indicates that Fe3O4 has been successfully modified. Fig. 1D describes the infrared spectra of Fe3O4 and T-MMIPs. The absorption peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1089 cm−1 are attributed to CO-NH and Si-O, respectively. Where the peak intensity of peak at 1089 cm−1 in T-MMIPs is significantly higher than that in Fe3O4, indicating the successful coating of SiO2 on Fe3O4. The absorption peak at 591 cm−1 can be due to Fe-O. In addition, the chemical bond and structure of synthesized boron doped carbon dots were also investigated with FTIR (Fig. 1E). The absorption at 3740 cm−1 comes from the stretching-vibration of O-H (Li, Li, et al., 2023) and the peaks at 3384 cm−1 and 3183 cm−1 are ascribed to the group of -NH2. The peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 are due to the stretching-vibration of C=O (Zhang, Ren, Ji, & Fan, 2020) and caused by C-N (Li, Zhao, et al., 2022), respectively. The vibration of BOH bond at 1106 cm−1 indicates the presence of B element in the carbon dots (Nechvolodova et al., 2017; Xu et al., 2023). Fig. 1F exhibits the specific XRD patterns of Fe3O4 and TMMIPs, and it was obvious that the characteristic peaks of TMMIPs were basically consistent with that of Fe3O4, which indicated that the modification with temperature-sensitive polymers would not change the lattice structure of Fe3O4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and fluorescence quenching mechanism of boron doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2A shows that the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak of the B-CDs changed with the change of excitation wavelength, but the position did not change. Due to its obvious excitation wavelength independence, the fluorescence of B-CDs may be attributed to surface state emission. The fluorescence intensity of B-CDs reached the largest value with excitation wavelength at 320 nm, and so the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments was set at 320 nm. Fig. 2B shows the effect of pH on the fluorescent characteristics of B-CDs. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs increased with the rise of pH value and reached the strongest value at pH 4, while decreased with pH value over pH 5–8, and then kept stable value over pH 8–14. The fluorescence intensity was very weak under alkaline conditions, which may be due to the deprotonation of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on B-CDs, resulting in the change of their surface charge, and thus affecting their fluorescence. Moreover, the stability of B-CDs was studied, and which was exhibited in Fig. 2C and D. The fluorescence signal of B-CDs can rapidly stabilize after 2 min, indicating that fluorescence probes based on B-CDs can realize rapid detection. Meanwhile, the fluorescence signal of B-CDs did not change significantly within 15 days, which demonstrated that B-CDs possessed unexceptionable stability.

The quenching mechanisms of CDs can be mainly classified into dynamic quenching, static quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and internal filtration effect (IFE) (Molaei, 2020), etc. As static quenching was concerned, the absorption spectrum of CDs is changed (Liu et al., 2017). In contrast, the absorption spectrum of CDs is unchanged in dynamic quenching mechanism (Zu et al., 2017). In addition, the fluorescent lifetime of CDs is changed. As FRET was concerned, the fluorescent spectrum of CDs and the absorption spectrum of quenching reagent might overlap, and the fluorescent lifetime of CDs declined. While IFE was concerned, the excitation or emission spectra of CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum of the quenching reagent, and the fluorescent lifetime of CDs remains almost unchanged (Li, Liu, et al., 2023; Li, Zhou, et al., 2023).

To investigate the fluorescence quenching mechanism of B-CDs by OTC, the UV–visible absorption spectrum, absorption spectrum, emission spectrum of B-CDs and the UV–visible absorption spectrum of OTC were studied. The results were exhibited in Fig. 2E and F. In Fig. 2E, curves a, b and c represented the UV–visible absorption spectrum of B-CDs at a concentration of 14 mg L−1, UV–visible absorption spectrum of OTC at 2 mg L−1, and UV–visible absorption spectrum of the mixture of B-CDs and OTC. It can be seen that the addition of OTC did not generate change on the absorption spectrum of B-CDs. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs was not due to the static quenching mechanism. Meanwhile, it was apparent that the absorption spectrum of OTC overlapped with the excitation spectrum of B-CDs from Fig. 2E and F (curve b in Fig. 2F), so the quenching of B-CDs may be attributed to IFE or FRET. For further confirming the mechanism, the fluorescent lifetimes of B-CDs, and the mixture of B-CDs and OTC were analyzed. The results were shown in Fig. 2G, in which curve a and b were the fluorescent lifetime curves of B-CDs and the mixture of B-CDs and OTC, respectively. It was obvious that no significant discrepancy between B-CDs and B-CDs + OTC was found. The fluorescent lifetimes of B-CDs and B-CDs + OTC were 7.06 and 7.14 ns, respectively. These indicated that the presence of OTC did not significantly alter the fluorescent lifetime of B-CDs. Taking the above results into consideration, the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs was ascribed to IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of important parameters for enrichment
ce:section.ce:para: MSPE is a vital enrichment process of the target analytes, some parameters will affect the enrichment efficiencies of the target analytes and influence the detection sensitivity of the proposed method. In order to achieve the optimal parameters, impacts of important factors such as the elution process, adsorption process, and detection process on the enrichment efficiencies were examined. When a parameter is optimized, the other parameters remain unchanged. The initial conditions were as follows: the spiked concentration of oxytetracycline, 20 μg L−1; the sample volume, 50 mL; the adsorption time, 60 min; the solution pH, pH 7; elution solvent, 9 mL methanol; elution time, 9 min; adsorption and elution temperatures, 25 °C. The optimized parameters were employed in the next experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of kind of eluent, eluent volume and elution time
ce:section.ce:para: As magnetic solid phase extraction is concerned, the elution step is very significant which is very important for the method development. Hence it is very crucial to select a suitable eluting agent to efficiently elute oxytetracycline from the adsorbents. Herein five solvents and three mixed solvents such as methanol, acetone, acetonitrile, ethanol, chloroform, and mixtures of methanol and acetic acid (9:1, 8:2, 8:3, v/v) were investigated for the elution of oxytetracycline. As shown in Fig. 3A, methanol resulted in the best elution and ethanol followed, while acetonitrile and acetone resulted in the poor elution. This may be related to the solubility of oxytetracycline in these five organic solvents. Within the temperature range involved in these experiments (20–45 °C), the solubility of OTC in methanol was higher than that in other solvents (Zhao, Yang, & Liu, 2016), so the elution performance of methanol was the best. In addition, the elution performance of solutions with different ratios of methanol and acetic acid on oxytetracycline was explored. The data showed that the addition of acetic acid remarkably reduced the elution performance of methanol. Therefore, methanol was optioned as the eluent.

The eluent volume and elution time are also key factors affecting the enrichment efficiency. Too little eluent or too short elution time can lead to insufficient elution of OTC from the adsorbents, resulting in a low recovery. Excessive eluent leads to the waste of organic solvents, and prolonged elution time can raise time costs. Therefore, in order to select the appropriate eluent volume and elution time, various volumes of methanol and elution time were optimized. From Fig. 3B, it can be seen that when the volume of methanol was less than 10 mL, the recovery of OTC raised with the increase of methanol volume, and which achieved the maximum with 10 mL methanol for eluting. Continuing to increase the volume of methanol, the recovery of OTC remained basically unchanged. This was due to that the elution of OTC on the adsorbent had reached equilibrium when 10 mL of methanol was added, and further increasing the volume of methanol cannot improve the recovery of OTC. The optimization results of the elution time were depicted in Fig. 3C. It was obvious that the elution efficiency of OTC gradually increased with increasing elution time, reaching maximum at 12 min. These results showed that 10 mL methanol and 12 min of elution time were sufficient to elute oxytetracycline from T-MMIPs. In order to save solvent and time, and also ensure the recovery of OTC as high as possible, the optimal volume of eluent in subsequent experiment was set as 10 mL, and the optimal elution time was set at 12 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of adsorption time and amount of T-MMIPs
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of getting the best enrichment performance, adsorption time is a special factor in the enrichment process, which was optimized in the range of 20–100 min. The related results were depicted in Fig. 3D. The recovery of OTC increased with prolonging the adsorption time until reaching the adsorption equilibrium. The adsorption equilibrium was achieved at 60 min, and the best recovery was achieved. Further increasing the adsorption time, the enrichment performance kept almost unchanged. Therefore, the adsorption interval was set at 60 min for the subsequent experiments.

The adsorbent T-MMIPs dosage was a crucial parameter in the MSPE procedure and optimized over 50–100 mg. As found in Fig. 3E, the recovery of OTC increased accordingly when the amount of T-MMIPs increased from 50 to 80 mg. This was because the increase of the amount of T-MMIPs led to the increase of available active sites, which in turn allowed for the adsorption of more OTC. A further increase of the amount of T-MMIPs resulted in almost constant recovery of OTC, and which might be contributed to that the amount of OTC in the solution was fixed, and the adsorption had reached the equilibrium and no more OTC was able to bind to them despite more active sites were available with continuous increase of the amount of T-MMIPs. These results indicated that 80 mg T-MMIPs were sufficient to extract OTC from the solution. Therefore, the amount of T-MMIPs in the subsequent experiments was set at 80 mg.

Oxytetracycline is an acid-base amphoteric compound with amino and hydroxyl groups in the molecule. The pH change will affect the present form of OTC, which maybe influence its enrichment in the magnetic solid phase extraction process. In addition, pH also affects the surface charge of the adsorbents, that in turn affects the reciprocity between OTC and the adsorbents. Therefore, the sample pH is one of the crucial parameters influencing the enrichment efficiency of OTC. The sample pH was optimized in the range of pH 3–9, and which was depicted in Fig. 3F. It was apparent that the recovery of OTC resulted in a significant increase over pH 3–7, and decreased significantly when the sample pH was over pH 7–9. In addition, since the adsorbents were magnetic functional materials, the acidity of solution can damage the structure of the adsorbents leading to a decrease of OTC recovery. Therefore, the solution pH for the subsequent experiments was set at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of adsorption temperature and elution temperature
ce:section.ce:para: T-MMIPs are temperature-responsive molecular imprinting modified composites, and temperature may play a crucial role in the adsorption and elution processes of OTC. Herein, the influence of temperature on the enrichment of OTC by T-MMIPs was explored in the range of 25–40 °C. As found from Fig. 3G, the adsorption performance of OTC improved with rising temperature and obtained the optimal value at 32.5 °C. With continuous rise of temperature, the extraction efficiency of OTC declined sharply. It was because the internal blotting cavity of T-MMIPs expanded with temperature lower than LCST of T-MMIPs, which was favorable for OTC entry and transferring, making the adsorption performance of OTC better. However, the T-MMIPs shrinked observably and the blotting cavity collapsed when adsorption temperature exceeded the LCST of T-MMIPs, which was unfavorable for OTC to enter into the blotting cavity (Xiong et al., 2022). Therefore, the adsorption of OTC reached the maximum at 32.5 °C. As the elution process was concerned, the temperature would also affect the elution performance, which was explored in the range of 20–32.5 °C. Fig. 3H exhibits the change of the elution efficiency of OTC with the temperature in the range of 20–32.5 °C. At 20 °C, the blotting cavity cannot be completely extended, and which resulted in lower elution efficiency. With the increase of the temperature, the elution efficiency reached the maximum at 22.5 °C and then decreased significantly with continuing to increase the temperature. This may be due to the contraction of T-MMIPs at higher elution temperature than 22.5 °C, which inhibited the release of OTC in the blotting chamber, resulting in decrease of the elution efficiency of OTC. Therefore, 32.5 °C was employed for the adsorption and 22.5 °C was utilized for elution in the following experimental processes.

The sample volume is correlated with the folds of OTC enrichment, which is also related to the sensitivity of the proposed method. With the other parameters keeping constant, sample volume was optimized over range of 30–70 mL, and which were described in Fig. 3I. It was found that the recovery of OTC gradually increased with the sample volume raising up to 50 mL, and further kept almost constant with the sample volume continuing to increase. In order to achieve better adsorption efficiency and best recovery, the sample volume was fixed to 50 mL in the next experimental processes.

Actual water samples usually contain a large number of ions, which may have an effect on the extraction of OTC. Therefore, the influence of ionic strength on OTC extraction was simulated with addition of NaCl, and examined in NaCl concentration range of 0–10%(w/v), and which were exhibited in Fig. 3J. It was obvious that the highest recovery of OTC was obtained without addition of NaCl. The recovery decreased gradually with raising the amount of NaCl, which might be ascribed to the salting-in effect which promoted the dissolution of OTC in water and increase of viscosity decreasing the migrating rate of OTC, thus reducing the adsorption of OTC. In addition, the increase of Na+ concentration also inhibited the π-π interactions between T-MMIPs and OTC, resulting in decline of the adsorption capacity of the adsorbent (Gao et al., 2012). Therefore, NaCl was not added in followed experimental processes.

The presence of oxytetracycline (OTC) was able to significantly reduce the fluorescent response of B-CDs as exhibited mentioned above. However, the dosage of B-CDs and sample pH might influence the detection performance during the fluorescence detection. They should be optimized to achieve optimal fluorescence detection parameters. The fluorescence intensity difference F0-F (ΔF) of B-CDs was chosen as the signal, in which F0 was the fluorescent intensity of B-CDs solution without the addition of OTC, and F was the fluorescent intensity after the addition of OTC. From Fig. 4A, it was apparent that ΔF increased significantly with increasing dosage of B-CDs when B-CDs dosage was less than 14 mg L−1, and decreased with continuous rise of B-CDs dosage. This was because the amount of OTC was excessive and lacked sufficient B-CDs to interact with OTC molecules when the B-CDs dosage was too small. On the other hand, if the dosage of B-CDs was too large, its own self-absorption maybe appear, resulting in weakening of fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the optimal B-CDs concentration was determined to be 14 mg L−1.

There are rich of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of B-CDs, so pH has an effect on their fluorescent intensity. Fig. 4B depicts the impact of pH on the fluorescent response of B-CDs. The experimental results demonstrated that when the pH was too low, the excessive hydrogen ions in the aqueous solution induced the protonation of carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of B-CDs, which in turn inhibited the interaction between OTC and B-CDs, leading to an extent decrease of the fluorescent quenching of B-CDs. When the pH was higher than pH 6, the sensitivity of B-CDs to OTC was not significantly enough for detection due to their own low fluorescence intensity. Therefore, pH 4 was chosen as the optimal pH after comprehensive consideration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the established method
ce:section.ce:para: The methodological evaluation of the established MSPE-FL method for the detection of OTC was carried out under optioned experimental parameters. As shown in Fig. 5A and B, the ΔF values of B-CDs showed an excellent linearity (R2 = 0.998) in the OTC concentration range of 0.2–60 μg L−1, and the limit of detection for OTC was 0.1 μg L−1 (LOD = 3σ/s, n = 15). These data demonstrated that the established method owned high sensitivity and would be employed for measuring OTC at low level in various samples.

Some substances with similar structure with OTC may interfere with the analytical results of OTC. In this experiment, five other antibiotics were considered to investigate the selectivity of the established method. As exhibited in Fig. 5C, it was found that ciprofloxacin (CFX), lincomycin (LNC), chlortetracycline (CTC), tetracycline (TC) and enrofloxacin (EFX) resulted in very slight effect on the adsorption and detection of OTC, where the concentration of OTC was 10 μg L−1 and the concentration of interferents was 50 μg L−1, and all experiments were carried out under optioned parameters. The results demonstrated that the adsorption of OTC on T-MMIPs was significantly better than that of other antibiotics, inferring that the designed method of coupling T-MMIPs and B-CDs possessed excellent selectivity in the detection of OTC. Fig. 5D reflects the adsorption ability of T-MNIPs for these six antibiotics, which demonstrated that T-MNIPs resulted in low enrichment of OTC and other antibiotics, and exhibited universal enrichment ability to six antibiotics. Compared with the performance of T-MMIPs, it was apparent that molecular imprinting could significantly enhance the adsorption ability and selectivity of the materials to OTC.

Recently, there have also many researches focusing on the detection of oxytetracycline based on fluorescence techniques. Cai et al. used copper nanoclusters (CuNCs@PEI) with blue-green luminescence as fluorescent probe to establish a rapid detection method for OTC based on IFE. The method owned a LOD of 14.7 μg L−1 and was succeeded in detecting OTC in bovine serum, human serum and milk samples with spiked recoveries of 95.6–107.8% (Cai et al., 2022). Wang et al. synthesized a fluorescent sensor for selectively and sensitively detecting OTC by embedding Ce and N co-doped CDs (Ce, N-CDs) into metal organic framework material (ZIF-67) and integrating it with MIPs. The linear range of the constructed sensor was 0.05–20 μg mL−1, and the LOD was 15 μg L−1. The sensor was effectively utilized for quantitatively determining OTC in milk samples and exhibited commendable precisions and reproducibility (Wang et al., 2023). Other studies of fluorescence-based methods for detection of oxytetracycline were shown in Table 1SS. Compared to these reported methods, the proposed method integrated with T-MMIPs and B-CDs resulted in lower detection limit and higher selectivity, which derived from the specific recognition of the prepared B-CDs to OTC due to selective fluorescence quenching and the selective capacity of T-MMIPs to OTC, and the high enrichment capacity of T-MMIPs based on MSPE. Moreover, the utilization of T-MMIPs enabled more accurate identification of OTC in complex sample matrices, which facilitates the improvement of the selectivity of the established method. Hence, the integration of T-MMIPs and B-CDs owned capacious application prospect in the analysis of OTC in food samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the established method
ce:section.ce:para: Precisions and stability of the established method are vital parameters for reliable application. In present work, the intraday and inter-day precisions and stability of the method were analyzed under the optimal conditions. For intraday precision analysis, water samples spiked with OTC at 1, 2 and 5 μg L−1 were involved and analyzed for six times in a day. For the inter-day precisions, the water samples spiked with OTC at the abovementioned levels were analyzed once a day for six continuous days. The obtained results showed that the intraday and inter-day precisions for the three concentration levels of OTC water samples were 2.26%, 1.64% and 0.76%, and 2.34%, 1.42% and 0.95%, respectively. These demonstrated that the established method earned better reproducibility and stability.

In this experiment, iced black tea, jasmine green tea and green tea were collected and taken for validation of the proposed method. These samples were pretreated and spiked with OTC at 1, 2 and 5 μg L−1. The blank and spiked samples were analyzed to evaluate the reliability and precisions of the established method. The analytical data were exhibited in Table 1 . The results demonstrated that OTC was not detected in any of the three samples, and the fortified recoveries ranged from 94.2% to 104.9%. The experimental results indicated that the method possessed great potential prospect in detecting OTC in tea beverages.

In this work, T-MMIPs composites integrating with thermosensitive property, magnetic property and molecular recognition were designed and synthesized, and exhibited excellent recognition, selectivity and enrichment capacity for OTC. Boron-doped carbon dots that can specifically recognize OTC were fabricated with nicotinic acid and boric acid by a facile hydrothermal method. Based on T-MMIPs and B-CDs, an innovate method was established for high enrichment and sensitive measurement of trace OTC in tea beverages. Temperature change can effectively facilitate the adsorption and elution of OTC. Under the optimal conditions, OTC provided satisfied linear range of 0.2–60 μg L−1, and LOD was 0.1 μg L−1. The established method was verified with three tea beverage samples, and the spiked recoveries were very good in the range of 94.2–104.9%, which proved the reliability and applicability of the designed method, and also provided a new concept for the detection of trace OTC and other contaminants in the aqueous samples.

Zhi Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Shuangying Li: Methodology, Software, Writing – review & editing. Liushan Jiang: Methodology, Resources. Jingwen Niu: Resources, Software. Yue Zhang: Formal analysis, Investigation. Linchun Nie: Validation, Visualization. Qingxiang Zhou: Resources, Validation, Visualization, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Chunmao Chen: Resources, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21976211).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.138998.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, J., Yang, L., Liu, J. (2016). Determination and correlation of oxytetracycline hydrochloride solubilities in organic solvents and abnormal phenomenon analysis. Chin. J. Antibiotic., 41(03), 218-222. doi:10.13461/j.cnki.cja.005695.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zu et al., 2017

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: A review on mechanisms and applications
Microchimica Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zu, F., Yan, F., Bai, Z., Xu, J., Wang, Y., Huang, Y., Zhou, X. The quenching of the fluorescence of carbon dots: A review on mechanisms and applications. Microchim. Acta, 184, 1899-1914. doi:10.1007/s00604-017-2318-9
